attributes.stem: A 19-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with concerns about her eating habits. She reports episodes of consuming large amounts of food in short periods, followed by feelings of guilt and shame. To compensate, she induces vomiting or exercises excessively. These episodes occur three to four times a week and have been ongoing for the past four months. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her body mass index (BMI) is 22 kg/m2. She reports feeling dissatisfied with her body shape and is preoccupied with thoughts about food and weight.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Other specified feeding or eating disorder
attributes.explanation:
Bulimia nervosa is the most likely diagnosis. Bulimia nervosa requires recurrent episodes of binge eating (consuming large amounts of food in a short period, with a feeling of a lack of control) accompanied by compensatory behaviors to prevent weight gain. In this case, the patient reports binge eating episodes followed by self-induced vomiting and excessive exercise, which are textbook compensatory behaviors seen in bulimia nervosa. The frequency (3-4 times per week) and duration (4 months) of these episodes meet the diagnostic criteria for bulimia nervosa, which require binge eating and compensatory behaviors to occur at least once a week for three months.

Atypical anorexia nervosa is a condition where individuals meet all criteria for anorexia nervosa, excepting that despite significant weight loss, their BMI is within or above normal. While this patient reports body dissatisfaction, there's no mention of significant weight loss or food restriction, making this diagnosis less likely.

Binge eating disorder involves recurrent episodes of eating large amounts of food in a short period, accompanied by a sense of lack of control. However, do not engage in regular compensatory behaviours, unlike bulimia nervosa. These behaviours are evident in this case.

Purging disorder is characterized by recurrent purging behavior to influence weight or shape in the absence of binge eating. While this patient does engage in purging behavior, she also reports binge eating episodes, which are not present in purging disorder.

Other specified feeding or eating disorder is a diagnosis used when symptoms of an eating disorder cause significant distress or impairment but do not meet the full criteria for any of the specific eating disorder diagnoses. Given that this patient's symptoms meet the full criteria for bulimia nervosa, this diagnosis would not be appropriate.

It's important to note that individuals with bulimia nervosa often maintain a normal body weight, which can make the diagnosis challenging without a thorough history. The normal BMI in this case is consistent with bulimia nervosa. Management typically involves a combination of psychotherapy (particularly cognitive-behavioral therapy), nutritional counseling, and sometimes medication (such as selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors).
learningPoints: Bulimia nervosa is characterized by recurrent episodes of binge eating with accompanying compensatory behaviors to prevent weight gain.
The diagnosis of bulimia nervosa requires binge eating and compensatory behaviors to occur at least once a week for three months.
Patients with bulimia nervosa often maintain a normal body weight, making the diagnosis challenging without a thorough history.
references: American Psychiatric Association DS, American Psychiatric Association DS. Diagnostic and statistical manual of mental disorders: DSM-5. Washington, DC: American psychiatric association; 2013 May 22.

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
